channel.title: NYT > Science
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Leaks on Space Station Lead Astronauts Briefly to Seek Shelter in Spacecraft
item.description: Russian astronauts prepared to make repairs on Friday morning, but after an hour and a half, officials decided no urgent action was needed.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: A Crew Dragon spacecraft docked with the International Space Station in 2022. NASA’s current crew of five was ordered to seek “safe haven” in a Crew Dragon while Russian astronauts repaired an air leak.

item.title: The New World Screwworm Is Back. Here’s What That Means.
item.description: Here’s why farmers and scientists are concerned over the New World screwworm’s unwelcome return to the United States.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes
item.media:description: The screwworm fly, whose larvae feed on the tissue of livestock, wildlife and pets. Its infections can kill if left untreated.

item.title: In a First, Scientists Precisely Edit Human Embryo Genes
item.description: Researchers relied on a newer gene-editing technique that may make it possible to engineer embryos, a prospect that has long alarmed bioethicists.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer
item.media:description: Researchers altered genes that influence levels of LDL cholesterol and hemoglobin in the blood.

item.title: NASA Says Goodbye to its Longtime Mars MAVEN Mission
item.description: The space agency announced that the MAVEN spacecraft, which has circled Mars for more than a decade, is being decommissioned.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:description: An artist’s concept of the Mars Atmosphere and Volatile Evolution, or MAVEN, which has been orbiting around Mars since 2014.

item.title: Gwynne Shotwell, Elon Musk’s No. 2 at SpaceX, Is the Company’s Steady Hand
item.description: Gwynne Shotwell, the president and chief operating officer, is the adult-in-the-room foil to Mr. Musk as SpaceX prepares for a blockbuster initial public offering.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac
item.media:description: Gwynne Shotwell, SpaceX’s president and chief operating officer, in Miami Beach last year during one of her infrequent public appearances. “I love working for Elon,” she has said.

item.title: Urban Light Pollution Might Be Worsening Allergies
item.description: Light pollution prompts plants to shed pollen longer and stronger, according to new research.
item.dc:creator: Marta Zaraska
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja for The New York Times
item.media:description: A study found that trees in cities with brighter lights start producing pollen earlier in the spring and finish later in the fall, adding up to 130 days per year to the allergy season.

item.title: The Toll of a ‘Missing Scientists’ Conspiracy Theory on the Families Left Behind
item.description: The claim that their loved ones’ deaths and disappearances are linked is almost certainly false — but the loss remains real.
item.dc:creator: Alexander Nazaryan
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Rebecca Noble for The New York Times
item.media:description: Elizabeth Weiss, an anthropologist whose husband, Nick Pope, a prominent U.F.O. researcher, died in April. “If you start looking for patterns, you will find them,” she said, quoting him.

item.title: As A.I. Makes Strides in Mathematics, Mathematicians Urge Caution
item.description: A week after OpenAI made headlines with an A.I.-generated proof, a new “declaration” by 16 experts raises concerns that the technology threatens math as a discipline.
item.dc:creator: Siobhan Roberts
item.media:credit: Aaron Wojack for The New York Times
item.media:description: OpenAI is just one of the artificial intelligence firms that has begun training its models in higher mathematics.

item.title: As Ebola Spreads, Scientists Race to Find Vaccines and Treatments
item.description: The Bundibugyo virus, a little known type, previously had caused just two small outbreaks. Now it’s at the center of a rapidly widening epidemic in Africa.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Health workers disinfected a coffin containing the body of a man presumed have died of Ebola in the Democratic Republic of Congo.

item.title: ANWR Oil Draws Few Bids, Despite Trump’s Push for ‘Liquid Gold’
item.description: An auction to drill in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge ended with just 10 percent of the available land claimed for oil development.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Christopher Miller for The New York Times
item.media:description: Caribou on the tundra of the 1002 area of the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge.

item.title: U.S. Forest Service to Open Millions of Acres to Off-Road Vehicles
item.description: The forthcoming order echoes President Trump’s decision to repeal executive orders that have protected other public lands from vehicles for decades.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Paul Ratje for The New York Times
item.media:description: The memo directs the U.S. Forest Service to identify which closed “roads, trails, areas, airstrips and waterways” in each national forest might be considered for year-round recreation access.

item.title: Trump Announces $700 Million in Funds Meant to Boost Coal Industry
item.description: The president announced a total of $700 million in federal money to reinvigorate the domestic coal industry, which has been in decline for decades.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and Brad Plumer
item.media:description: Of the roughly $700 million in total funding, $425 million would be used to upgrade and extend the life of 13 coal plants that otherwise might have closed in the coming years.

item.title: Bernard Roizman, Virologist Who Demystified Herpes, Dies at 96
item.description: He mapped the herpes simplex virus genome, revealing how it invades cells. His work also helped lay the groundwork for potential vaccines and gene therapies.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman

item.title: Arizona, Nevada Agree to Trade for Desalinated Pacific Ocean Water
item.description: A decade-old treatment plant in San Diego County, Calif., could leave more water in the Colorado River for states facing severe shortages.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Sandy Huffaker for The New York Times
item.media:description: From left, Dan Denham of the San Diego County Water Authority; Scott Cameron of the U.S. Bureau of Reclamation; Tom Buschatzke of the Arizona Department of Water Resources; and Colby Pellegrino of the Southern Nevada Water Authority toasted the agreement at the Carlsbad Desalination Plant in California on Wednesday.

item.title: E.U. Steps Up Ocean Monitoring as Trump Administration Backs Away
item.description: Days after the U.S. said it would kill a network of ocean monitors, European officials pledged to invest more in their version, calling it a “necessity.”
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.media:description: The European plan to invest roughly $107 million comes as the U.S. has said it would dismantle a sprawling network of sensors.

item.title: Unusual Greenpeace Lawsuit May Proceed, Dutch Court Says
item.description: Greenpeace International is arguing under Dutch law that an American pipeline company, Energy Transfer, sought to silence it with a costly lawsuit in North Dakota.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Alyssa Schukar for The New York Times
item.media:description: Demonstrators against the Dakota Access Pipeline in North Dakota in 2016. The lawsuits are centered on Greenpeace’s involvement in the protests.

item.title: Democrats Pledge to Fight Trump’s Termination of Ocean Monitoring Observatories
item.description: The Trump administration is dismantling a $368 million deep-ocean observation system that monitors marine ecosystems and the effects of climate change.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: National Science Foundation Ocean Observatories Initiative
item.media:description: Recovering a surface buoy — part of a network of moorings, buoys and gliders making observations of the ocean — from the Irminger Sea off Greenland’s coast, in 2021.

item.title: New York Sues Trump Administration Over TotalEnergies Wind Farm
item.description: The lawsuit argues that it is illegal to pay the French energy giant TotalEnergies $795 million to cancel a planned wind farm off New York.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:description: Turbines are visible at Sunrise Wind offshore wind farm under construction off the coast of Montauk Point, N.Y.

item.title: Crested Ibises Get the Royal Treatment in Japan as They Fly Again
item.description: Once widespread in Japan, the colorful birds went from being fairly commonplace in the country to being on the verge of extinction.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:description: Two crested ibises were released into the wild during a ceremony attended by Japan’s crown prince and princess on Sunday.

item.title: Trump Administration to Dismantle the Ocean Observatories Initiative
item.description: The $368 million network of instruments collecting data in both the Atlantic and Pacific has been critical to climate and ocean research.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.media:description: A mooring that was used in the Ocean Observatories Initiative was recovered after operating for a year in the Gulf of Alaska.

item.title: Giant Stone Urns Hint at the Death Rites of a Lost People in Laos
item.description: Thousands of mysterious containers lie scattered across northern Laos. These “death jars” may have provided a form of communal interment, archaeologists reported.
item.dc:creator: Franz Lidz

item.title: 2025 Wildfires Were the Costliest Ever, Researchers Say
item.description: Severe, hard-to-control blazes in densely populated areas like Los Angeles drove the year’s record losses.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Dzombak
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pacific Palisades, Calif., on Jan. 7, 2025. Fires in the Los Angeles area last year burned at least 90 square miles, killing at least 31 people and displacing more than 150,000.

item.title: The Blue Micromoon Rises in Sunday’s Early Skies
item.description: The second full moon of May will look smaller and dimmer than usual.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:description: A micromoon rising over North Macedonia last year. A blue moon is when a second full moon occurs in a single calendar month, while a micromoon is when it is at its farthest distance from Earth.

item.title: By September, Nearly a Third of Americans Will Live in States With Legal Aid in Dying
item.description: Despite widespread support in polls, the number of people who actually go through with the practice remains very small.
item.dc:creator: Paula Span
